author: lOrm
content:
Semantic refinement on the strict helper: `check_tool_permission_strict` previously denied on *both* `None` mgr AND `mgr.is_enabled() == false`. After adding `PermissionManager::unrestricted()` (which is an explicitly-disabled sentinel meaning 'allow everything'), the strict helper would have contradicted the new contract — calling `execute_tool_with_permission_required(..., &PermissionManager::unrestricted())` would have returned Denied, making the explicit allow-all unreachable.

Fix: strict now denies ONLY when `permission_mgr` is `None`. A disabled manager is an explicit allow-all override per the library contract. The renamed test `strict_permission_allows_when_manager_is_explicitly_disabled` locks this in. The original regression test `strict_permission_denies_when_manager_absent` (None case) is unchanged. This keeps the bypass-risk surface tight: the only way to 'accidentally allow' is to forget to supply a manager, which is exactly what the refactor is designed to catch.
